content_id:
Inti dari teologi as-Sanusi dalam Umm al-Barahin adalah konsep tanzih — transendensi ilahi, atau ketidakserupaan mutlak Allah dengan makhluk-makhluk. Konsep ini mendorong seluruh pembahasan tentang sifat-sifat ilahi dan menjelaskan posisi-posisi spesifik yang diambil dalam persoalan-persoalan yang diperdebatkan antara tradisi kalam Asy'ari/Maturidi dan pendekatan tradisionalis Athari.

Bagi as-Sanusi dan tradisi kalam yang ia wakili, menegaskan bahwa Allah sepenuhnya transenden berarti bahwa setiap sifat yang dilekatkan kepada Allah harus dipahami dengan cara yang menghilangkan segala kemiripan dengan sifat-sifat makhluk. Ilmu Allah tidak seperti ilmu manusia, yang diperoleh dan terbatas. Kekuasaan Allah tidak seperti kekuasaan manusia, yang beroperasi melalui upaya fisik dan terkendala oleh keadaan. Pendengaran dan penglihatan Allah tidak seperti pendengaran dan penglihatan manusia, yang memerlukan organ-organ fisik dan terbatas oleh jarak dan medium. Sifat-sifat ilahi adalah nyata — as-Sanusi secara tegas menegaskannya — namun nyata dengan cara yang sepenuhnya melampaui realitas yang diciptakan.

Komitmen terhadap transendensi ini mempengaruhi cara as-Sanusi memperlakukan deskripsi-deskripsi Qur'ani tertentu tentang Allah. Ayat-ayat yang menyebut wajah Allah, tangan-Nya, atau mata-Nya; yang menggambarkan Allah bersemayam di atas 'Arsy; atau yang menyebut Allah datang pada Hari Kiamat — semua ini memerlukan interpretasi yang cermat, menurut as-Sanusi, untuk menjaga transendensi ilahi. Langkah interpretatif yang tepat bervariasi menurut kasus: sebagian sifat ia tegaskan sambil menekankan perbedaan sempurna realitasnya dari analogi-analogi makhluk; sebagian lain ia interpretasikan dengan cara yang menghindari segala kesan spasialitas atau embodyment ilahi.

Di sinilah tradisi kalam dan tradisi Athari berbeda paling signifikan. Posisi Athari berpendapat bahwa deskripsi-deskripsi Qur'ani ini harus diakui sebagai sifat-sifat nyata tanpa reinterpretasi, sambil mengingkari kemiripan apa pun dengan makhluk dan menahan diri dari bertanya bagaimana caranya. Tradisi kalam, termasuk as-Sanusi, berpendapat bahwa komitmen terhadap transendensi ilahi mengharuskan bahwa deskripsi-deskripsi tertentu tidak dapat dipahami dalam makna lahiriahnya — bahwa menegaskan Allah memiliki wajah, tangan, atau lokasi spasial secara harfiah akan merupakan bentuk tasybih yang tidak sesuai dengan monoteisme yang benar.

As-Sanusi menyajikan posisi kalam dengan sangat jelas dan berargumen untuknya secara cermat dalam Umm al-Barahin. Ia tidak meremehkan teks-teks yang dinukil tetapi mengembangkan metode interpretatif yang ia yakini menghormati otoritas kitab suci dan keniscayaan rasional dari transendensi ilahi sekaligus. Para ulama Asy'ari dan Maturidi sebagian besar berbagi pendekatan ini, meskipun dengan beberapa perbedaan dalam di mana mereka menarik garis interpretatif pada pertanyaan-pertanyaan spesifik.

Bagi pelajar yang menemukan perdebatan ini, penting untuk mengenali bahwa tradisi kalam yang diwakili as-Sanusi maupun tradisi Athari sama-sama berkomitmen pada prinsip yang sama: Allah sepenuhnya tidak serupa dengan makhluk-makhluk. Ketidaksepakatan mereka adalah tentang apa yang diperlukan prinsip itu secara interpretatif ketika diterapkan pada deskripsi-deskripsi tertentu dalam Al-Qur'an dan hadis.
content_en:
At the heart of as-Sanusi's theology in Umm al-Barahin is the concept of tanzih — divine transcendence, or the absolute dissimilarity of God from created things. This concept drives the entire treatment of divine attributes and explains the specific positions taken on questions that were disputed between the Ash'ari/Maturidi kalam tradition and the Athari traditionalist approach.

For as-Sanusi and the kalam tradition he represents, affirming that God is absolutely transcendent means that any attribute applied to God must be understood in a way that eliminates any resemblance to created attributes. God's knowledge is not like human knowledge, which is acquired and limited. God's power is not like human power, which operates through physical effort and is constrained by circumstances. God's hearing and sight are not like human hearing and sight, which require physical organs and are limited by distance and medium. The divine attributes are real — as-Sanusi emphatically affirms them — but they are real in a manner that transcends created reality entirely.

This commitment to transcendence affects how as-Sanusi treats certain Quranic descriptions of God. Verses that speak of God's face, hands, or eyes; that describe God as settling on the Throne; or that speak of God coming on the Day of Judgment — all of these require careful interpretation, in as-Sanusi's view, to preserve divine transcendence. The appropriate interpretive move varies by case: some attributes he affirms while insisting on the complete difference of their reality from created analogs; others he interprets in ways that avoid any suggestion of divine spatiality or embodiment.

This is where the kalam tradition and the Athari tradition differ most significantly. The Athari position holds that these Quranic descriptions should be affirmed as real attributes without reinterpretation, while denying any resemblance to creation and refraining from asking how. The kalam tradition, including as-Sanusi, holds that the commitment to divine transcendence requires that certain descriptions cannot be taken in their apparent sense — that affirming God has a literal face, hands, or spatial location would constitute a form of anthropomorphism incompatible with proper monotheism.

As-Sanusi presents the kalam position with great clarity and argues for it carefully within Umm al-Barahin. He is not dismissive of the transmitted texts but rather develops an interpretive method that he believes honors both the authority of scripture and the rational necessity of divine transcendence. Both Ash'ari and Maturidi scholars largely shared this approach, though with some differences in where they drew interpretive lines on specific questions.

For students encountering this debate, it is important to recognize that both the kalam tradition represented by as-Sanusi and the Athari tradition are committed to the same foundational principle: God is utterly unlike created things. Their disagreement is about what that principle requires interpretively when applied to specific descriptions in the Quran and hadith.

This concept drives the entire treatment of divine attributes and explains the specific positions taken on questions that were disputed between the الأشعري/الماتريدي علم الكلام التراث and the الأثري traditionalist approach. For as-Sanusi and the علم الكلام التراث he represents, affirming that God is absolutely transcendent means that any attribute applied to God must be understood in a الطريق that eliminates any resemblance to created attributes. God's العلم is not like human العلم, which is acquired and limited. God's power is not like human power, which operates through physical effort and is constrained by circumstances. God's hearing and sight are not like human hearing and sight, which require physical organs and are limited by distance and medium. The divine attributes are real — as-Sanusi emphatically affirms them — but they are real in a manner that transcends created reality entirely. This commitment to transcendence affects how as-Sanusi treats certain القرآني descriptions of God. الآيات that speak of God's face, hands, or eyes; that describe God as settling on the Throne; or that speak of God coming on the يوم القيامة — all of these require careful interpretation, in as-Sanusi's view, to preserve divine transcendence. The appropriate interpretive move varies by case: some attributes he affirms بينما insisting on the complete difference of their reality from created analogs; others he interprets in ways that avoid any suggestion of divine spatiality or embodiment. This is where the علم الكلام التراث and the الأثري التراث differ most significantly. The الأثري position holds that these القرآني descriptions should be affirmed as real attributes without reinterpretation, بينما denying any resemblance to creation and refraining from asking how. The علم الكلام التراث, بما في ذلك as-Sanusi, holds that the commitment to divine transcendence requires that certain descriptions cannot be taken in their apparent sense — that affirming God has a literal face, hands, or spatial location would constitute a form of anthropomorphism incompatible with proper monotheism. As-Sanusi presents the علم الكلام position with great clarity and argues for it carefully within Umm al-Barahin. He is not dismissive of the transmitted texts but rather develops an interpretive method that he believes honors both the authority of scripture and the rational necessity of divine transcendence. Both الأشعري and الماتريدي العلماء largely shared this approach, though with some differences in where they drew interpretive lines on specific questions. For students encountering this debate, من المهم to recognize that both the علم الكلام التراث represented by as-Sanusi and the الأثري التراث are committed to the same foundational principle: God is utterly unlike created things.
